text.title: Pyramid Principle, SCQA, and Pixar storytelling frameworks
text.summary: Three complementary structures for business narratives: Pyramid Principle for logic-driven documents, SCQA for building narrative tension, and Pixar for case studies and change stories.

rule.statement: Lead with the main point (answer/recommendation), then provide 2-4 supporting arguments, each backed by evidence. Arguments must be MECE: Mutually Exclusive (no overlap) and Collectively Exhaustive (nothing missing).
rule.rationale: Busy readers get the answer immediately and can stop when satisfied. Bottom-up structure forces readers to process all evidence before reaching a conclusion they may reject.

example.note: Cloud migration proposal using Pyramid Principle
example.code:
RECOMMENDATION: Migrate to cloud infrastructure by Q3.
ARGUMENT 1: 40% lower TCO Evidence: Internal TCO analysis vs current on-prem costs Evidence: Competitor benchmarks showing 35-45% savings
ARGUMENT 2: Eliminates current capacity ceiling Evidence: Current servers at 87% utilization peak Evidence: Projected 3x traffic growth in 18 months
ARGUMENT 3: Reduces deployment cycle from 2 weeks to 2 days Evidence: Deployment time comparison with cloud-native competitor Evidence: Developer survey: 68% cite deployment speed as top blocker
Background: Our servers are aging... Analysis: We compared three options... Findings: Cloud showed cost savings... Conclusion: We recommend migrating to cloud.

rule.statement: Open with a Situation the audience accepts as true, introduce a Complication that creates tension, raise the Question that tension produces, then deliver the Answer. Never invent a complication where none exists.
rule.rationale: SCQA creates narrative tension that makes the audience want the answer before it is given. Artificial complications undermine credibility.

example.note: SCQA for a product quality proposal
example.code: S: Our team ships 10 features per quarter — strong velocity. C: But 30% of features have critical bugs in the first week, eroding customer trust. Q: How do we maintain velocity while improving quality? A: Implement automated testing with an 80% coverage gate before any feature ships.

section.title: Pixar Framework

rule.statement: Use the Pixar structure for case studies and change initiative stories: Once upon a time [situation] / Every day [routine] / One day [change] / Because of that [consequence 1] / Because of that [consequence 2] / Until finally [resolution].
rule.rationale: The cause-effect chain of "Because of that" forces specificity about what the intervention actually changed, unlike generic "before/after" formats.

example.note: Pixar framework applied to a process automation story
example.code: Once upon a time, our team spent 10 hours/week on manual reporting. Every day, they copy-pasted data between 5 spreadsheets. One day, we implemented automated dashboards. Because of that, reporting time dropped to 1 hour/week. Because of that, the team redirected 9 hours to analysis instead of data entry. Until finally, they identified $500K in cost savings in Q4.

section.reference: Case study template using Pixar structure
